problems.stem: You're choosing between a free concert ticket and a paid job that pays \$80 for the same evening. The concert is worth \$50 of enjoyment to you. The opportunity cost of going to the concert is:

options.comment: The ticket's price isn't the opportunity cost.
That's the *value* of the concert, not its cost.
Correct. The next-best alternative is the \$80 job.
Don't double count.

problems.solution: Opportunity cost is the value of the next-best alternative *given up*. The job paying \$80 is what you forgo. The ticket's price (\$0) is irrelevant; \$50 is the *benefit*, not the cost.

problems.stem: Given $y = 100 - 5x$, find $y$ when $x = 12$.
Explain what it means to think "at the margin" in two or three sentences, with a brief example.
problems.model_answer: Thinking at the margin means evaluating the *next* unit of an activity — the additional benefit and the additional cost of one more unit — rather than averages or totals. Example: when deciding whether to study one more hour, compare what that hour adds to your exam score against what it costs you in sleep or leisure, not your average return per hour studied this semester. Rational choices set marginal benefit equal to marginal cost.
key_points: Marginal = the *next* unit, not the average.
Compare marginal benefit to marginal cost.
Concrete example with MB and MC named.
Optimum: MB = MC.

problems.stem: Which of these would shift the demand curve for new cars to the *right*?

options.text: A drop in the price of new cars
options.comment: That's a movement *along* the demand curve, not a shift.
For *complements* like gasoline, a price rise shifts the related good's demand left.

options.text: A rise in average household income (cars are a normal good)
options.comment: Correct. Higher income shifts demand for normal goods right.

options.text: A rise in the price of used cars
options.comment: Used cars are a *substitute*; their price rise *would* shift new car demand right. (Trick option — both C and D could plausibly do it.)

problems.solution: Demand shifters: income, prices of related goods, tastes, expectations, number of buyers. For a *normal* good, income up → demand right. (D is actually also a valid right-shift via substitution; in a single-answer question, treat the most direct income effect as the intended answer.)

problems.stem: The demand for a good is $Q_d = 100 - 5P$. At $P = 8$, the quantity demanded is:
problems.solution: Substitute $P = 8$: $Q_d = 100 - 5(8) = 100 - 40 = 60$. Movement *along* the curve, not a shift.

problems.stem: Briefly explain the difference between a *shift in demand* and a *movement along the demand curve*.
problems.model_answer: A *movement along* the demand curve is the change in quantity demanded caused by a change in the good's *own price* — everything else held constant. A *shift in demand* is a change in the whole curve caused by something *other than* the good's price: income, tastes, substitutes' prices, expectations, number of buyers. Same curve vs. different curve.
key_points: Movement along: caused by own-price change.
Shift: caused by everything else (income, related goods, tastes, etc.).
Same curve vs. different curve.

problems.stem: True or false: A drop in the price of an input (say, flour for a bakery) shifts the supply curve for bread to the *right*.

options.comment: Correct. Lower input cost makes production cheaper at every quantity; supply shifts right.
Lower input cost reduces production cost — supply shifts right.

problems.solution: Input cost is a non-price determinant of supply. Lower input cost means the firm is willing to supply more at every price — the curve shifts right. New equilibrium: lower price, higher quantity.

problems.stem: A supply curve is $Q_s = 2P + 20$. At $P = 30$, the quantity supplied is:
Briefly explain why a supply curve typically slopes upward.
problems.model_answer: Higher prices make producing each unit more profitable, so existing firms produce more and new firms enter. Above some threshold, marginal cost also rises as a firm pushes its current capacity (overtime wages, less-productive resources). The two forces together — entry plus rising marginal cost — give the curve a positive slope.
key_points: Higher price → higher profit on each unit → more produced.
Marginal cost rises as output increases.
Entry of new producers at higher prices.

problems.stem: Demand for concert tickets is $Q_d = 200 - 4P$ and supply is $Q_s = 20 + 2P$. The equilibrium price is:

options.comment: At \$20, $Q_d = 120 > Q_s = 60$. Shortage.
Correct. At \$30, both equal 80.
At \$40, $Q_d = 40 < Q_s = 100$. Surplus.
Surplus widens further.

problems.solution: Set $Q_d = Q_s$: $200 - 4P = 20 + 2P \Rightarrow 180 = 6P \Rightarrow P = 30$. At $P = 30$, $Q = 80$ on both sides.

problems.stem: Using $Q_d = 200 - 4P$ and $Q_s = 20 + 2P$, what is the equilibrium quantity?
problems.solution: Equilibrium price is $P = 30$ (solve $Q_d = Q_s$). Substitute back: $Q = 20 + 2(30) = 80$, matching $Q_d = 200 - 4(30) = 80$.

problems.stem: If demand shifts right and supply doesn't change, what happens to equilibrium price and quantity? Explain in one or two sentences.
problems.model_answer: A rightward demand shift moves the new equilibrium up the supply curve: equilibrium *price* and *quantity* both rise. Buyers want more at every price; suppliers respond by producing more, but only at higher prices.
key_points: Both price and quantity rise.
Movement along the supply curve.

problems.stem: A per-unit tax is imposed on a market. Which side ends up bearing the larger share of the tax?

options.comment: Tax incidence depends on elasticities, not who legally pays.
Same — depends on elasticities.
Correct. The side that can least substitute away bears more.

options.text: Whichever side is legally required to remit the tax
options.comment: Statutory vs. economic incidence are different; economic incidence depends on elasticities.

problems.solution: Statutory incidence (who writes the check) is irrelevant. *Economic* incidence is determined by relative elasticities: the more inelastic side has fewer alternatives and bears the larger share.

problems.stem: A city sets a binding price ceiling on apartment rents. In two or three sentences, identify the most likely *short-run* and *long-run* effects.
problems.model_answer: Short run: the ceiling produces a shortage at the capped price — waiting lists, key money, non-price rationing emerge. Long run: depressed returns reduce new construction and accelerate conversion to non-rental uses, shrinking the housing stock and deepening the shortage. Current tenants benefit; future renters and overall housing quality lose.
key_points: Short-run: shortage + non-price rationing.
Long-run: reduced supply (less new building, more conversion).
Distributional: current tenants gain at others' expense.

problems.stem: Use a supply-and-demand diagram (described in words) to explain *deadweight loss* from a per-unit tax. Identify the regions of consumer surplus, producer surplus, government revenue, and DWL on the new equilibrium.
problems.model_answer:
Start at no-tax equilibrium ($P^*, Q^*$) where consumer surplus (CS) is the triangle above $P^*$ and below demand, and producer surplus (PS) is the triangle below $P^*$ and above supply.

Impose a per-unit tax $t$. The buyer price rises to $P_b$, the seller price falls to $P_s$, with $P_b - P_s = t$. Quantity drops from $Q^*$ to $Q'$ where buyers' willingness to pay equals sellers' willingness to accept plus $t$.

At the new equilibrium:
- *New CS* is the smaller triangle above $P_b$ and below demand (up to $Q'$).
- *New PS* is the smaller triangle below $P_s$ and above supply (up to $Q'$).
- *Government revenue* is the rectangle of width $Q'$ and height $t$.
- *Deadweight loss* is the triangle between $Q'$ and $Q^*$, bounded by the demand and supply curves — the value of trades that no longer happen because the tax wedge made them unprofitable.

DWL grows with elasticities and with the square of the tax rate.
key_points: Tax wedges $P_b$ above $P_s$ by $t$; quantity falls from $Q^*$ to $Q'$.
CS and PS both shrink; their losses are bigger than government revenue.
DWL is the triangle of foregone trades.
DWL grows with elasticities and with $t^2$.

problems.stem: When demand is *inelastic*, an increase in price causes total revenue to:

options.comment: Correct. Inelastic demand: $|\varepsilon| < 1$, so quantity falls proportionally less than price rises, lifting revenue.
That's the *elastic* case ($|\varepsilon| > 1$).
Constant revenue requires unit elasticity ($|\varepsilon| = 1$).
It can be determined directly from elasticity.

problems.solution: Total revenue is $P \times Q$. Inelastic demand means $|\varepsilon| < 1$: quantity falls by *less* in percent than price rises, so revenue goes up. Elastic ($|\varepsilon| > 1$): revenue falls. Unit elastic ($|\varepsilon| = 1$): revenue unchanged.

problems.stem: The price of a concert ticket rises from \$40 to \$50. Quantity demanded falls from 200 to 160. Using the simple percent-change formula, the price elasticity of demand (absolute value, one decimal):
Why is the demand for insulin much less elastic than the demand for a specific brand of soda? Briefly explain.
problems.model_answer: Insulin is a life-sustaining medication with no close substitutes for the people who need it — they will pay almost any price, so quantity demanded barely responds to price changes (highly inelastic). A specific soda brand has many close substitutes (other sodas, juices, water); a price increase pushes buyers easily to alternatives, so quantity demanded falls sharply (elastic).
key_points: Necessity vs. luxury.
Close substitutes drive elasticity.
Insulin: no substitutes → inelastic. Soda brand: many substitutes → elastic.

problems.stem: A profit-maximizing firm hires labor up to the point where:

options.text: Average product of labor equals the wage
options.comment: That's not the profit-maximizing rule.

options.text: Marginal revenue product of labor equals the wage
options.comment: Correct. $\text{MRP}_L = w$ is the standard hiring rule.
Minimizing labor cost ignores the revenue side.

options.text: The marginal product of labor is zero
options.comment: That would mean hiring past usefulness.

problems.solution: $\text{MRP}_L = \text{MP}_L \times P$ (in a competitive output market) is the additional revenue from hiring one more worker. Hire iff $\text{MRP}_L > w$; stop at $\text{MRP}_L = w$ to exhaust all profitable hires.

problems.stem: A firm hires labor up to where $\text{MRP}_L = w$. In two or three sentences, explain why that's the right stopping point.
problems.model_answer: Each additional worker generates revenue equal to her marginal product times the output price ($\text{MRP}_L$). Hiring her is profitable iff that exceeds her wage. The firm keeps hiring while $\text{MRP}_L > w$ and stops at $\text{MRP}_L = w$, which exhausts all profitable hires without making unprofitable ones.
key_points: $\text{MRP}_L = \text{MP}_L \times P$ in competitive output markets.
Hire while marginal revenue > wage.
Equality at the margin is the optimum.

problems.stem: Country A produces 10 cars or 100 wheat per worker. Country B produces 5 cars or 80 wheat per worker. Comparative advantage in wheat belongs to:

options.text: Country A, because it has higher absolute productivity in both
options.comment: Absolute productivity is irrelevant to comparative advantage.

options.text: Country B, because its opportunity cost of wheat is lower
options.comment: Correct. B gives up $5/80 = 0.0625$ cars per bushel; A gives up $10/100 = 0.10$.
They differ: 0.10 vs 0.0625.
Comparative advantage is defined from production possibilities alone.

problems.solution: Comparative advantage goes to whoever has the *lower opportunity cost*. B's cost per bushel of wheat ($\frac{5}{80} = 0.0625$ cars) is lower than A's ($\frac{10}{100} = 0.10$). B specializes in wheat, A in cars; both gain.

problems.stem: A country imposes a tariff on imported sugar. In two or three sentences, identify the main winners and losers.
problems.model_answer: Winners: domestic sugar producers (face less competition, get a higher price) and the government (collects tariff revenue). Losers: domestic sugar consumers (pay more) and foreign sugar producers (lose market share). Net welfare typically falls — the consumer loss exceeds the producer-plus-government gain, leaving deadweight loss.
key_points: Winners: domestic producers + government.
Losers: domestic consumers + foreign producers.
Net DWL is the standard result.

problems.stem: A factory pollutes a river downstream without paying for the damage. The free-market equilibrium produces:

options.comment: Externalities push the private optimum away from the social optimum.

options.text: Too little output, because demand is suppressed
options.comment: The factory's *private* cost is below social cost — output is too *much*, not too little.

options.text: Too much output, because the factory ignores external damages
options.comment: Correct. MSC > MB at the private optimum; the extra units generate DWL.

options.text: Zero output, because regulation will eventually shut it down
options.comment: Without intervention, the factory keeps producing.

problems.solution: When production imposes uninternalized costs on third parties, the factory's marginal social cost (MSC) exceeds its marginal private cost. At the private profit-max quantity, MSC > MB. Output is above the efficient level; the excess generates deadweight loss.

problems.stem: A factory emits pollution that lowers nearby property values. (a) Explain why the free market produces too much pollution. (b) Describe two distinct policy instruments to correct it. (c) Say what determines the choice between them.
problems.model_answer:
**(a)** The factory's private cost is lower than the social cost: pollution imposes damages on neighbors that the factory doesn't pay for. At the private-cost-minimizing quantity, marginal social cost exceeds marginal benefit. Output is above the socially efficient level, generating DWL.

**(b)** Two standard instruments: a *Pigouvian tax* equal to marginal external damage at the efficient quantity, internalizing the externality into the factory's cost; and a *cap-and-trade* permit system that fixes total emissions and lets firms trade permits.

**(c)** Choice depends on what's better known. A tax is preferred when marginal damage is known better than abatement cost — it pins down the price of pollution and lets quantity adjust. A cap is preferred when the absolute level matters (a damage threshold) and abatement cost is the bigger unknown. Cap-and-trade has political appeal via grandfathered permits; a tax produces revenue and is administratively simpler.
key_points: MSC > MB at private optimum; output above efficient level; DWL.
Pigouvian tax internalizes external cost.
Cap-and-trade fixes quantity, lets permit price adjust.
Choice depends on which is better known: damages vs. abatement cost (Weitzman 1974).
Bonus: revenue, political feasibility, distribution.

problems.stem: Which of these is a *defining* feature of a perfectly competitive market?

options.text: Firms set prices to maximize profit
options.comment: All firms maximize profit; perfect competitors take prices as given.
That's monopolistic competition.

options.text: Many firms each selling a homogeneous product, with free entry and exit
options.comment: Correct. Many sellers, identical product, free entry/exit, perfect information.

options.text: Barriers to entry protect incumbent profits
options.comment: That's monopoly or oligopoly.

problems.solution: Perfect competition: (i) many small firms, (ii) homogeneous product, (iii) free entry/exit, (iv) perfect information. Implication: each firm is a price taker; long-run profit driven to zero by entry.

problems.stem: Why is long-run economic profit zero under perfect competition? Explain in two or three sentences.
problems.model_answer: If incumbent firms are earning positive economic profit, new firms enter (free entry assumption). Entry expands supply, pushes price down, and continues until each firm earns zero economic profit. Similarly, persistent losses drive exit until those remaining break even. Long-run profit is zero because nothing prevents the entry-and-exit margin from grinding it away.
key_points: Free entry/exit is the mechanism.
Positive profit attracts entry; losses cause exit.
Process continues until economic profit is zero.

flashcards.definition: The value of the next-best alternative given up when a choice is made.
flashcards.why: The only cost concept that consistently shows up in real decisions — money, time, attention. If you don't compare alternatives, you're not doing economics.
Tells you whether revenue moves with price the same direction (inelastic) or opposite (elastic), and who bears a tax. Two numbers, lots of predictions.

flashcards.definition: The loss in total surplus when the market quantity is pushed away from the efficient quantity by a tax, ceiling, floor, or other intervention.
flashcards.why: Quantifies the cost of distorting prices. Bigger elasticities mean bigger DWL for a given tax — which is why we tax inelastic goods like gasoline.

flashcards.definition: A cost or benefit of a transaction that falls on a third party who isn't involved in the trade.
flashcards.why: Externalities are the canonical reason markets *fail* to deliver the socially efficient quantity. Most environmental and public-health policy is some flavor of trying to internalize them.

flashcards.definition: The *next* unit. Marginal benefit (MB) is the value of one more unit; marginal cost (MC) is what it costs. Optimum: MB = MC.
flashcards.why: Most economic decisions are 'how much,' not 'whether.' The margin is where the real action is.

flashcards.definition: A producer has a comparative advantage in a good if its *opportunity cost* of producing that good is lower than the other producer's.
flashcards.why: Trade is mutually beneficial whenever comparative advantages differ — even if one side is absolutely better at everything. Pattern, not levels.
